text.content:
Welcome, first-time DM. This module is built so you can focus on running a great Dark Souls night instead of juggling rules. Here is everything you actually need to do. On first launch the module offers to import and arrange everything and pin your macros — just say yes.

The 60-second pitch to your players

"You are Unkindled — restless dead who refuse to stay down. You will die tonight. A lot. That is the point. Death sends you back to the last bonfire a little wiser; the world never changes, but you do. Read your enemies, learn their tells, and you will win. This is Dark Souls at the table."

What to prep before session one

  1. Read the Soulslike Rules journal once (you can skim the cheat sheet). Players only strictly need the Defensive Reaction System page.
  2. Read Run-of-Show (next page) so you know the spine of the night.
  3. Read Running the Defensive System — this is the one new thing you operate at the table.
  4. Skim the four boss Moveset Cards and their Tell Ladders (in the Bestiary / DM Codex).
  5. Have players pick pregens (Pregen Characters compendium). No character building required.

The only few things you operate

  • The Souls pool — one shared number on the Party sheet. Award it when they kill things; spend it when they level/shop. Macros do the math.
  • The bonfire loop — when they rest: full heal, respawn enemies, offer leveling. That's it.
  • The windowed defense — when an enemy attacks, narrate three beats and let the target react. (Full how-to two pages on.)
  • Cinders & the shops — click Spend a Cinder when a hero falls; after each boss, click Boss Defeated to award souls and grow the Firelink shop. Both are one-button macros; the lives sit on the Bonfire Ledger.

Golden DM rules for this game

  • Never say "you die." The world is deadly; let their choices spring the trap. (See Teaching Deaths.)
  • Telegraph everything. Big danger always gets a visible wind-up. Fair beats surprising.
  • Reward reads, not stats. When a player correctly calls a tell, lean in and let it land hard.
  • Let them win when they earn it. If a clever party trivializes a fight by reading it perfectly — that is a triumph, not a problem.

Your one-glance reference for the night of: prep, the first words out of your mouth, and a pacing clock so you never have to wonder “am I behind?”

15 minutes before they arrive

  • Open the world. On first launch Ashen offers to set everything up — click Yes (you'll then see the Ashen — folders). If you skipped it, run the Assemble Adventure macro.
  • Drag 1 · Cemetery of Ash onto the canvas and activate it.
  • Open two journals in side tabs: the DM Handbook (this) and the DM Codex: Bestiary (the boss cards).
  • Have the Pregen Characters folder ready; one actor per player.
  • Confirm the Bonfire Ledger exists; souls read 0 / 0 / 0 (setup does this).
  • Run New Run / Reset Bonfire and enter your player count — it sets the party's Cinders (4 × players) and Shop Tier 0.
  • Your tracker macros are already on the hotbar (setup pins them): Award / Bank / Spend Souls, Bonfire Rest, Boss Defeated, Spend a Cinder, Drop Bloodstain, New Run / Reset. Missing? Re-run Assemble Adventure.

The first three things you say

  1. The pitch. Read the blockquote from the Quick-Start page: “You are Unkindled. You will die tonight. That is the point…”
  2. The one rule they need. “When something attacks you, I describe it in three beats. On your beat you choose — Dodge, Block, or Parry. Reading the enemy IS the game; you’ll learn their tells by getting hit.”
  3. The table-talk permission. “You each decide your own action, but talk freely — call out tells, plan, backseat each other. Co-op is encouraged.”

Then run the tutorial hollows and let someone drop to 0 HP early, so the death rules land while the stakes are cheap.

The pacing clock (4–6 hour night)

ActTargetHard cutoffIf you’re behind
1 · Cemetery + Gundyr75 min90 minTrim tutorial hollows to one wave.
2 · Firelink hub45 min60 minDeliver the hook fast; cap shopping time.
3 · High Wall120 min135 minSkip the Dragon; one courtyard wave only.
4 · Vordt + epilogue60 min75 minRun Vordt at the lower HP target.

If you’re running long (triage in this order)

  1. Skip the Dragon entirely — it is explicitly optional.
  2. Cut Pus of Man (High Wall) and route straight to the pre-Vordt bonfire.
  3. Trim courtyard farming to a single clear.
  4. Run Gundyr and Vordt at their lower HP targets (Codex → “Running Bosses”).

End of night

  • When Vordt falls: read the epilogue, drop the Undead Settlement sequel hook, fade to black.
  • Banked souls and character state persist — if the group wants a Part 2, you’re already set up.

The night is four acts, roughly 4–6 hours. Times are guides — compress by trimming hollows, expand by leaning into farming and the dragon.

Act 1 — Cemetery of Ash (60–75 min)

  1. Awakening. The PCs claw out of their graves as Unkindled. Cold open — no exposition dump. They start at level 3.
  2. Tutorial hollows. Crawling hollows, then a sword hollow. Teach the four base actions (Strike, Dodge, Block, Parry), Estus, and that death is not the end. Let someone drop and recover so the death rules land.
  3. Cemetery bonfire. First rest. Show them the loop: heal, refill Estus, respawn enemies.
  4. IUDEX GUNDYR — first major boss, 2 phases. He is the forgiving teacher: clean, readable moveset. Reward → level 4–5.

Act 2 — Firelink Shrine (hub, 30–45 min)

A breather and spend-souls hub. All four NPCs are available:

  • Fire Keeper — level up (spend banked souls).
  • Shrine Handmaid — shop (Estus shards, catalysts, consumables).
  • Andre — upgrade weapons and the Pyromancy Flame with titanite.
  • Sword Master — optional duel + the summon-sign mercy valve for later.

Deliver the lore + plot hook, then warp to the High Wall.

◆ Two-shot break (recommended): end session one here, at Firelink. Cinders carry over; grant +2 — “the fire stirs” — at the session-two cold open. See Two-Shot — The Firelink Break.

Act 3 — High Wall of Lothric (90–120 min)

  1. High Wall bonfire (entry).
  2. Hollow Courtyard. Hollow soldiers; this is the designated soul-farm pocket (respawns on rest) for struggling tables. → L5–6.
  3. The Dragon. Optional secret boss on the rampart — three resolution paths (bypass / drive off / hunt to kill). See its card.
  4. OUTRIDER KNIGHT — mini-boss 1, a fast brutal duel that punishes turtling. → L6–7.
  5. Shortcut unlock. A lift/ladder loops back to the bonfire.
  6. PUS OF MAN — mini-boss 2; a "dead" hollow erupts into an abomination. Fire-weak. → L7–8.
  7. Pre-Vordt bonfire — right outside the fog gate. Zero run-back.

Act 4 — Climax (45–60 min)

  1. VORDT OF THE BOREAL VALLEY — final boss, 2 phases. Frostbite build-up, wild charges, frost-brittle joints weak to fire/thunder. At 50% HP he frenzies (the exam). → L8–10.
  2. Epilogue. The frost lifts; the gate to the Undead Settlement opens. Sequel hook, fade out.

If you are running short on time

  • Skip the Dragon (it is explicitly optional).
  • Trim the Hollow Courtyard to one wave; only farm if the party is underleveled.
  • Cut Pus of Man and route straight to the pre-Vordt bonfire.
  • Run Gundyr and Vordt at the lower HP targets.

If you have extra time

  • Run the full Dragon hunt (path C) for a real secret-boss payoff.
  • Let them farm the courtyard and over-level — Vordt is more fun when they feel strong and STILL have to read him.
  • Lean into Firelink roleplay with the NPCs.

Ashen is written as a one-shot, but it plays beautifully as a two-shot — and most first-time tables will want the extra room. The session break is built into the structure: end session one at Firelink Shrine, the hub at the close of Act 2.

Why Firelink is the break

Firelink is the save point — it’s where they level (spend banked souls), shop, upgrade, and take the plot hook before warping to the High Wall. Ending there is a natural “to be continued”: warm, safe, and forward-looking.

  • Session 1 = Acts 1–2. Awaken → learn the windowed defense on tutorial hollows → Iudex Gundyr → reach Firelink, light the bonfire, first real level-up, meet the four NPCs, take the hook. End on the warp prompt.
  • Session 2 = Acts 3–4. The High Wall gauntlet (Dragon, Outrider, Pus) → Vordt.

Why the split is even (it doesn’t look it)

Acts 1–2 are less content but slower — everyone’s learning Dodge/Block/Parry windows, Estus rationing and the death loop. Acts 3–4 have more fights but run faster because the table has the system in hand. So the two halves feel about equal even though session two has more on paper. Lean into it: session one is the tutorial that doesn’t feel like one; session two is the payoff.

Ending session one

Hit the Firelink beat hard: the bonfire flares, the Fire Keeper greets them, the first level-up lands (send them home thinking about their build), deliver the hook, then end on the choice to warp. If the group decides one session was enough, Gundyr + Firelink is already a complete arc — they woke, learned the world, beat the forgiving teacher, found the hub. Nobody leaves mid-fight.

Opening session two (cold open)

  • Open at the Firelink bonfire, fire low. No exposition dump.
  • 60-second recap — what they did, where they are, what the hook was.
  • Confirm builds — everyone re-reads their level-up and Estus count.
  • “The fire stirs”: +2 Cinders. A small mercy that marks a fresh session without erasing the stakes — Cinders otherwise carry straight over from session one.
  • Then warp to the High Wall and go.

Running a strict single one-shot instead? Skip the break, cut the optional Dragon, and take the hard time-cuts in Run-of-Show. The two-shot is the relaxed default; the one-shot is the speedrun.

pages.name: What Each Fight Hunts (and the Counter)

text.content:
Every major fight in Ashen quietly hunts a habit — a playstyle or character build it is designed to punish. This is your at-a-glance prep table: know what each fight preys on, then coach the counter through the Tell Ladder (never by stating it outright).

FightHunts (the habit / build it preys on)Counter to coach
Iudex GundyrThe reckless and the relieved — players who panic-block his clean swings, and a party that celebrates the "win" on an empty Estus.Parry his honest swings; keep one flask and a held action for the eruption.
Pus of Man (from Gundyr)Resource-drained, clustered parties; punishes spending your last flask on a boss that is not fully dead.Hold resources, spread out, and bring fire — it is fire-weak.
Outrider KnightThe turtle. Shield-campers and passive, defensive builds — it hastes when ignored and pounces the most defensive PC.Stay aggressive (deal damage every round) and sidestep the charge instead of walling it.
Pus of Man (High Wall)Fireless parties in tight formation who stroll past "corpses." A shock ambush on the unprepared.Carry a firebomb or pyromancy; don’t bunch into the poison cone.
The DragonThe DPS racer. Glass cannons and brave open-ground play — you cannot out-damage it from the open bridge.Positioning over damage: advance cover-to-cover into the dead zone, then strike.
VordtThe clusterer. Bunched parties that ignore the status bar — the frenzy charge shreds a cluster and Frostbite stacks into Exhaustion.Spread out, bait the charge out of the lane, manage Frostbite, and hit the frost-brittle joints with fire/thunder.

Why this matters for your table

The build triangle — bruiser / turtle / caster — means no single party answers every fight the same way. The turtle that walls Gundyr gets devoured by the Outrider; the caster who races the Dragon down gets torched. Let each fight expose a different weakness, and let the party adapt at the bonfire. That adaptation is the game.

pages.name: What to Explain vs. Let Them Discover

text.content:
The fastest way to kill the Dark Souls feeling is to explain the puzzle. The fastest way to confuse your table is to hide the chassis. Here is the line: teach the machine, hide the answers.

Explain up front — the chassis (they cannot infer these)

  • Bonfires are the only rest. Resting heals fully, refills Estus and spell charges, respawns regular enemies, and is where you level.
  • Estus is your healing flask — limited charges, refilled only at a bonfire.
  • Souls are one shared pool that is both money and XP. Earn them from kills; spend them to level and shop. The macros track it.
  • The death loop. Dying is not the end: you respawn at the last bonfire and drop your carried souls as a bloodstain. Reach it to recover them; die again first and they are gone.
  • The windowed defense exists. Every attack has three beats, and on your beat you pick Dodge / Block / Parry. Teach that the system exists and how to declare — not which answer is right.
  • Table-talk is allowed. Each player owns their own choice, but advice and planning are encouraged.
  • They start at level 3 and play pregens — no character building required.

Let them discover — the game itself

  • Which window is the right answer for each move — the core puzzle. They learn it by getting hit and from your Tell Ladder hints after a wipe.
  • That each boss hunts a build or habit (turtling, clustering, racing for damage). Don’t announce it — let the fight expose it. (See “What Each Fight Hunts.”)
  • Unblockable attacks. Never pre-warn “you can’t block that.” Let a walled charge hurt once, then ladder the read.
  • Enemy weaknesses (the Pus and Vordt fear fire; Vordt also hates lightning). Reward the player who tries it rather than telling them.
  • The Gundyr eruption — that the “victory” is not a victory. Let it ambush them exactly once.
  • The Dragon’s three paths (bypass / drive off / hunt to kill). Present the situation, not the menu.
  • Parry timing and ripostes — the thrill is in nailing it themselves.

Never say

  • “You die.” The world is deadly; their choices spring the trap. (See Teaching Deaths.)
  • The boss’s HP number, or “it’s almost dead.” Describe wounds, not bars.
  • The right answer to a tell before a death has earned the lesson.
  • “You can’t do that” about a defense — let them try, let it fail honestly, then ladder the hint.

Rule of thumb: if it is something a returning Souls player would simply know about the table (bonfires, Estus, souls), explain it. If it is something a player earns by playing (the read, the weakness, the opening), let them find it.

This is the one new system you operate live. It is simple once you have run two attacks. Here is the DM-side procedure.

On an enemy's turn, for each attack

  1. Pick the move (boss) or use the enemy's single move (mook). Open its Moveset Card.
  2. Narrate the three windows out loud, in order, with a beat between them: W1 wind-up, W2 commit, W3 impact. Make the telegraph vivid — that is the fairness.
  3. Ask the targeted player: "How do you answer — Dodge, Block, or Parry, and on which window?"
  4. Look up that cell on the card's Defensive Profile grid → it tells you ADV / normal / DIS / impossible.
  5. They roll one flat d20 (no ability mod) at that quality vs the move's DC. Resolve.

How to narrate windows (examples)

"Vordt hauls the great mace up over his shoulder, frost steaming off it (W1)… he steps in and the head comes screaming down in an overhead arc (W2)… it's about to crater the stone where you stand (W3). What do you do?"

Players declare against whichever window they think is the opening. The grid does the rest.

Resolving each defense

Dodge (free)Flat d20 vs Dodge DC. Success: no damage, move them 5 ft; if behind the enemy, their next attack has advantage. Failure: full damage.
Block (costs reaction)No roll, deterministic: subtract the shield's set % per damage type (its card). Chips at low tiers; never staggers. Charges / grabs / shield-kicks are unblockable — they show — on the Block row.
Parry (costs reaction)Flat d20 vs Parry DC; only the ideal window is ADV, all else DIS. Success: negate + stagger the enemy (it loses its next action) and the whole party gets a riposte (free attack, treat as advantage/crit-friendly). Failure: full damage.

Keeping it fast

  • Only the target reacts. For an AoE move, each creature in the area reacts (Dodge only, usually).
  • A declaration is one phrase plus one d20. Don't let it become a negotiation.
  • Mooks have ONE move — after the first exchange the table already knows the answer, so trivial fights fly by.
  • Don't reveal the grid. Reveal it through play and through Tell Ladders on wipes.

Impossibility tags

Each move lists what cannot beat it: unblockable (grabs, magic), undodgeable (tracking charges — block or eat it), unparryable (anything not a clean weapon swing). Enforce these — they are what make each move a distinct puzzle.

Calibrating difficulty on the fly

  • Too easy? Use the boss's nastier moves and its legendary actions; tighten DCs by 1–2.
  • Too hard / table frustrated? Volunteer a Tell Ladder rung early, soften a DC, or have the Sword Master summon sign appear.

pages.name: Running Battles — What to Keep Track Of

text.content:
This is the one thing you actively operate at the table. After two or three attacks it becomes muscle memory. Here is everything you track and the loop you repeat.

Before a fight starts (10 seconds)

  • Drop the enemy / boss token(s) on the active scene.
  • Open the boss’s Codex card (movesets + window answers + Tell Ladder) and its statblock (HP, phase trigger).
  • Note the phase threshold if any — Gundyr: at 0 HP he erupts; Vordt: at 50% he frenzies.

What you keep an eye on each round

TrackWhereWhy
Boss HPtoken / statblockPhase triggers & “is it nearly over.”
Current move + windowCodex cardTo narrate the three beats and resolve the read.
Frostbite stacks (Outrider / Vordt)Add Frostbite Stack macroWhen it fills → Exhaustion.
Estus remaining (each PC)player sheetsThe resource-drain tension.
Souls on the lineBonfire Ledger (carried)What a wipe will cost them.
Anti-turtle flag (Outrider)a coin / your memoryDid anyone damage it last round?
Reaction used (each PC)per turnBlock/Parry share one; Dodge is free.
Legendary actions (bosses)statblockExtra jabs between PC turns.

The attack loop (repeat for every enemy attack)

  1. Name the move and narrate beat 1 (the wind-up). “He hauls the halberd overhead…” This is the telegraph — always give it.
  2. The target declares a reaction — Dodge, Block, or Parry — and which window they commit to. Table-talk is fine.
  3. Resolve against the card. The move’s grid gives ADV / normal / DIS / X for that window; apply it to the d20.
  4. Narrate the outcome as a wound or a whiff, never a number. On a clean parry: stagger + a free party riposte.

Resolving the three defenses (deterministic — no extra rolls)

  • Dodge — the universal answer. ADV on the right window, DIS on the wrong one. Always available, unlimited, free.
  • Block — read the card’s Block row: — ordinary (reduces normally), half a lesser shield halves it, chip a heavy hit still chips through, X unblockable (a raised guard does nothing). No Strength save, ever. Costs the shared reaction.
  • Parry — ADV only on the move’s parry window (usually the commit/impact beat), DIS elsewhere, X if unparryable. Success staggers and opens a riposte. Costs the shared reaction.

The reaction economy: Block and Parry share one reaction per round; Dodge is always free. A PC with Endurance/Poise 5+ earns a second reaction. Don’t sweat exactness — the rule exists so that turtling has a cost.

Phase & special triggers to watch

  • Gundyr → Pus of Man: at 0 HP he does NOT die — swap in the Pus actor and continue. Telegraph the eruption.
  • Vordt at 50%: he frenzies — add the cold aura, Frostbreath Cone, and Grab-Leap. This is the exam.
  • Outrider anti-turtle: if no PC damaged it last round, it gains an extra action this round. Track one yes/no flag.
  • Dragon gauntlet: there is no damage race in the open — PCs Dash cover-to-cover (a flat d20 vs the breath timer) to reach the safe dead zone.

After the fight

  • Run Award Souls for the kill (targets live on the “Bonfires, Souls & Death” page).
  • If they wiped: run Drop Bloodstain, reset the boss and area, advance the Tell Ladder one rung, respawn them at the bonfire.
  • At a bonfire: run Bonfire Rest (heal / refill / respawn) and offer leveling.

Minimum viable tracking: the boss’s HP, which reaction each PC has spent this round, and any Frostbite stacks. Everything else is on the Codex card in front of you.

Print this page, or keep it open in a side tab — the whole fight loop on one card.

The attack loop

  1. Name the move & narrate beat 1 (the wind-up = the telegraph — always give it).
  2. Target declares Dodge / Block / Parry and which window they commit to.
  3. Resolve vs the boss card grid: ADV / normal / DIS / X on the d20.
  4. Narrate a wound or a whiff — never a number. Clean parry → stagger + free party riposte.

Block legend (deterministic — no Str save)

—ordinary — blocks normally
halfa lesser shield halves it
chipheavy hit still chips through
Xunblockable — a raised guard does nothing; step out instead

Reactions

  • Dodge — free & unlimited. ADV on the right window, DIS on the wrong one.
  • Block & Parry share one reaction per round. Endurance/Poise 5+ → a second reaction.
  • Parry — ADV only on the parry window; success = stagger + riposte.

Don’t forget

  • Frostbite stacks (Outrider / Vordt) → Exhaustion when the bar fills.
  • Phase triggers: Gundyr at 0 HP → Pus of Man; Vordt at 50% → frenzy.
  • Outrider anti-turtle: no PC damaged it last round → it gains +1 action.
  • On a wipe: Drop Bloodstain, reset the boss/area, advance the Tell Ladder one rung.
  • Cinders (lives): spend 1 when a fallen hero rises or on a wipe — they never refill at a bonfire; at 0, the next fall is real.

Track only: boss HP · each PC’s reaction this round · Frostbite stacks, and the party’s Cinders. Everything else is on the boss card.

When the party rests at a bonfire

  1. Everyone to full HP; refill all Estus and spell charges; clear Exhaustion and build-up.
  2. Respawn all non-boss enemies in the area (reset their tokens). Bosses stay dead.
  3. At the Firelink bonfire only: offer leveling via the Fire Keeper (spend banked souls → apply a Level-Up Card). Other NPCs are available here too.

Awarding souls

Use the Award Souls macro (adds to the Party pool) whenever they kill something. Targets:

Common hollow30–60
Elite100–150
Mini-boss (Outrider, Pus)1,000
Gundyr2,500
Vordt3,000
Dragon (kill, path C)3,000–4,000 + unique drop

Level costs (spent at Firelink)

500 / 800 / 1,200 / 1,800 / 2,600 / 3,600 / 5,000 souls for levels 4 → 10. Boss kills alone cover roughly four levels; farming and the dragon cover the rest.

Death — two cases

One PC hits 0 HPNormal 5e death saves. Allies can stabilize/heal. The fight continues. Nothing is lost.
Whole party down (wipe)Respawn everyone at the last bonfire. Reset the boss and area enemies. Run the Drop Bloodstain macro: their carried souls become a bloodstain token at the death spot, and you advance the boss's Tell Ladder by one rung.

The bloodstain

Place a bloodstain token where they fell. If they reach it, run Reclaim Bloodstain (returns the souls). If they wipe again first, delete the old bloodstain (gone forever) and drop a new one. Don't soften this — the threat of loss is the engine of caution.

The mercy valve

After several wipes on the same wall, drop the Sword Master summon sign near the fog gate. If used, place his controllable phantom token to fight alongside them (DS3 co-op). He fades when the boss dies or the party rests.

Cinders are the party’s shared lives pool (the players’ rules page covers the fiction). This page is how you set and run them. Track the count on the Bonfire Ledger actor’s Primary Resource bar.

Set the pool: 4 per player

Cinders scale with table size so the night plays the same whether you have three players or six. Set the count at the start of session one:

Players3456
Cinders12162024

One shared pool. Spend 1 whenever a fallen hero is pulled back up, or on a full wipe. They never refill at a bonfire — Cinders only go down, and that falling number is your tension gauge.

The teaching deaths are priced in

The four scripted teaching deaths (see The Four Teaching Deaths) draw Cinders like any fall — that’s tuition, and it’s baked into the 4-per-player budget. Tell players up front that dying is part of learning here, so the cost never feels like a gotcha. Roughly four Cinders go to teaching; the rest absorb their own mistakes.

Read the gauge (rough spend map)

  • Gundyr + the Pus eruption: ~3–4
  • Outrider (mini): ~2–3
  • High Wall Pus (mini): ~2–3
  • The Dragon (optional): ~2–3 if they take it
  • Vordt (finale): ~5–7

A competent party reaches Vordt with a small cushion; a sloppy one arrives on fumes. If they hit Vordt under 5, telegraph harder and ease off — you want the climax to land. With 10+, press them: make the exam feel like an exam.

What zero means

Hitting 0 Cinders should escalate, not end the night. Mid-run, treat it as the world turning crueler — push them back hard, and make a fall cost the slow way (5e death saves, an ally’s heal). Reserve true failure for Vordt: out of Cinders at the exam is the perfect last stand — win now, or the Flame goes out. (You can always quietly hand back a Cinder if a great run is about to die to bad dice; the number is yours to steer.)

pages.name: The Four Teaching Deaths (Run Them Fair)

text.content:
Lethality is designed, never scripted. You never declare a death; the world is genuinely dangerous and the party's own choices spring the trap. In hindsight, every death was avoidable.

The five rules of a fair Souls death

Before any near-certain death lands, all five should be true:

  1. Telegraphed — they could see it coming (a cue, scorched corpses, the boss rears back to inhale).
  2. Self-inflicted — their choice sprang it (charged the bridge, got greedy, clustered up).
  3. Soft consequence — death is respawn + dropped souls, not a dead character.
  4. Legible lesson — it points at exactly one fixable mistake.
  5. Exploitable in hindsight — the thing that killed them is the key to winning.

The four moments (each teaches a transferable skill)

1. Gundyr Phase 2The party "wins," relaxes, low on Estus — then the Pus of Man erupts from his corpse and mauls them. Lesson: victory isn't victory until the bar's empty; save resources.
2. The DragonThey charge the open rampart and a full-bridge fire breath torches the cluster. Lesson: positioning over DPS; use cover, get behind it.
3. Outrider KnightA shield-camping, passive party gets ground down by relentless pressure (he hastes if not attacked). Lesson: aggression and footwork beat turtling.
4. Vordt Phase 2 (the exam)At 50% HP he frenzies: a wild charge through the cluster plus frostbite stacks → Exhaustion → cascade. Lesson: spread out, manage the status bar, bait the charge.

How to spring one without forcing it

  • Lay the telegraph clearly, then let them choose. If they read it and play safe, reward that — no death required.
  • If they take the bait, let the consequence be real and total. Then, at the bonfire, make sure the lesson is legible (a Tell Ladder rung, an NPC line, an obvious environmental clue).
  • Never repeat the same teaching death twice. Once they've learned it, that danger becomes a tool they can exploit.

How Tell Ladders work

Each boss has a prepared Tell Ladder: a sequence of escalating hints about its exploitable openings (the Defensive Profile, its weakness, its rhythm). The party climbs the ladder by dying. Each wipe, hand them the next rung — always delivered in-fiction, never as a stat sheet.

  • Rung 1 is a nudge ("the corpses are all clustered mid-bridge").
  • Middle rungs name a concrete opening ("his overhead leaves him wide open right as it lands").
  • Final rung hands them the kill ("beneath it, the fire can't reach and the belly is soft").

Crucially, the Ladder gives knowledge, not power — no stat buffs, no power creep. The party gets stronger by understanding. Full ladders for each boss live on their cards in the Bestiary / DM Codex.

Two ways past every wall

If a party is stuck, remind them (in-fiction) they always have both options:

  • Get smarter — climb the Tell Ladder, read the moveset.
  • Get stronger — retreat, farm souls at the courtyard pocket, level at the Fire Keeper, return.

Scaling for party size (3–6)

3 playersDrop boss HP to the low end of its range; reduce legendary actions by one; thin mook waves. Push the Sword Master summon earlier.
4 playersRun as written (the baseline).
5–6 playersUse the high HP end; add 1 legendary action; add an extra hollow or two per wave; bosses may target two PCs per round with separate moves.

Adjusting on the fly

  • Watch Estus economy — if the party is consistently dry before bosses, add an Estus Shard to the courtyard loot or soften a wave.
  • If a fight is a cakewalk, that's fine for mooks; for a boss, escalate with its nastier moves rather than just inflating HP.
  • Keep the night moving toward Vordt — that climax is the payoff. Don't let early farming sprawl past your time budget.
